HOW DOES THE FED QE OR QT AFFECTS GLOBAL LIQUIDITY?
The Federal Reserve’s actions—quantitative easing (QE) or quantitative tightening (QT)—have a massive impact on global liquidity, which is basically the amount of money sloshing around in the world economy. Here’s how each works and why it matters,

The 10-year high-quality market corporate bond par yield represents the yield or interest rate on corporate bonds with a high credit rating and a 10-year maturity. These bonds are considered relatively safe and are issued by companies with a low risk of default.

Liquidity: The level of deposits in commercial banks reflects the liquidity available in the banking system. Higher deposit levels indicate a greater availability of funds for lending and investment purposes. It suggests that individuals and businesses have surplus funds and are willing to keep them

Decreased Lending Capacity: Banks use their reserves to extend loans to individuals and businesses. When reserves are tied up in Treasury deposits, banks have fewer funds available for lending. This reduction in lending capacity can lead to a decrease in overall liquidity in the economy.

Impact on Reserves and Money Supply: When the Treasury deposits funds in its account at a Federal Reserve Bank, it effectively removes those funds from circulation.
